title: Willow herb trug
subtitle: A shallow oval willow trug for harvesting herbs and salad leaves
excerpt: A shallow oval trug from brown buff willow, 380 mm long and 100 mm deep, with an arched handle and open rand sides that let fine soil drop through. The trug uses a flat oval base, a low randed body, and a single handle rod lashed across the top. A quick single-session basket project.
sourceNotes: Cooke The Basketmaker (1882) for the shallow trug form and handle insertion method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the kitchen garden herb-gathering trug.

glossaryTerms.definition: A willow rod used in basket-making. The arched handle of the trug is a thick withy bent over and lashed to the rim.
One of the upright structural rods forming the sides of the trug.
The cross of base rods from which the base spokes radiate, forming the foundation of the flat oval base.
Single-rod weave: one weaver passes in front of one stake and behind the next, producing an open, airy side wall.

content.text: A herb trug needs open sides: the
weave leaves gaps between each row that allow water to drain and let a gardener pick through the trug without handling every item in it. The arched handle
is made from a thicker rod, typically 6 to 8 mm, that bends without cracking when soaked. The flat oval
base takes about forty minutes; the sides and handle take another hour.

Brown buff willow in two sizes: thick rods 5 to 6 mm for the base stakes and the handle bow; fine rods 2.5 to 3 mm for the side weavers and the base tie weave. You need approximately 150 g of each size. Soak all rods for forty-five minutes and mellow in a damp cloth for twenty minutes before starting. The handle bow rod must be among the best-soaked: soak it for a full hour separately if possible.
Build an oval slath: four thick rods form the long axis; two thick rods cross through them at right angles. Tie the
with a pairing tie for two rounds, then open to individual spokes. Pair out to 380 mm long and 220 mm wide. Finish the base with two rows of three-rod waling to set the edge.

beside each base spoke. Kink each stake at the base edge with the bodkin, bend it upright, and tie all stakes together at the top. Complete the upsett: two rows of three-rod waling in each direction to lock the base edge and pull the stakes vertical.

weave from the upsett up. Weave 70 to 80 mm of side in rand, leaving visible open spaces between the rows. This openness is intentional. Finish with two rows of three-rod waling and then a three-rod plain border.

Take the thick handle bow rod and bend it into an arch over the trug. The arch should rise 120 mm above the rim at its peak and extend 100 mm below the rim at each end for the insertion. Pierce the weaving at each side of the trug, two stakes in from each end, using the bodkin. Insert the handle bow ends into these holes and push them down through the side weaving to the base level. Lash the handle to the rim with a thin withy: wrap four times around both the handle and the rim wire, then tie off.

Dry the trug after each garden use; storing it wet accelerates mould on the willow surface. A light brush with a dry cloth is all the cleaning needed. A willow herb trug used daily will last three to five seasons.

attrs.symptom: The handle bow cracked when bending
attrs.cause: Rod not soaked long enough or a knot in the bending zone
attrs.fix: Use the longest, knot-free rod in the bundle for the handle. Soak it for ninety minutes rather than forty-five. If it cracks, switch to a new rod; a cracked handle bow will break in use.

attrs.symptom: The base warps upward at the centre
attrs.cause: Base woven too tightly at the slath centre before opening
attrs.fix: Press the base flat on a board and weight it overnight while still damp. Wet the underside of the slath area if needed to re-soften.
